properties.Deskripsi: Aiq/Aik (Sasak: 'water/river') + Nyet (Sasak: local plant name, likely Ficus sp. or riparian herb); descriptive-natural biotic name — the river characterised by a specific bankside plant.
Air Terjun (ID: 'waterfall') + Grepek (Sasak: from grepek/gerpek, possibly 'to scratch/erode', referencing the erosion action of the falls on the rock face); morpho-geographic hydrological.
Air Terjun + Prabe (Sasak/Sanskrit: variant of praba/prabha 'radiance/light'); morpho-geographic waterfall, with a descriptive-luminous qualifier from Sanskrit.
Air Terjun + Segenter (Sasak village/place name; possibly from senter 'to funnel/channel'); morpho-geographic waterfall named for the valley location.
Tibu (Sasak: 'basin/plunge pool') + Atas (Malay/ID: 'upper/above'); morpho-geographic positional name — the upper waterfall basin.
Air Terjun + Timponan (Sasak: from timpoan/timboan 'to overflow/spill over'); morpho-geographic hydrological name describing the cascading character.
Bangko-bangko — Sasak reduplicated form, possibly from bangko (a type of driftwood stump or coastal shrub); morpho-geographic coastal landform name.
Batu (Malay: 'stone/rock') + Idung (Sasak: 'nose'); morpho-geographic shape descriptor — a rock formation resembling a nose.

Batu ('stone/rock') + Kijuk (Sasak: the barking deer, Muntiacus muntjak); morpho-geographic name referencing a rock associated with deer — boundary between MG and DN; classified MG as the rock (landform) is the naming anchor.
Bukit (Malay: 'hill') + Ledu (Sasak: possibly from ledu, a type of thorny shrub or from 'to clear/open land'); morpho-geographic hill name.

Bukit ('hill') + Tiga Rasa (ID: 'three tastes/flavours'); descriptive-natural sensory name — the hill is named for three distinct spring-water tastes (sweet, salty, sour) at its base, encoding gustatory ecological knowledge.
Buwun (Sasak: 'spring/well/water source') + Mas (Malay/ID: 'gold'); morpho-geographic hydrological name with a colour/value qualifier referencing golden mineral-rich water or gold-mining history.
Centra Kerajinan (ID: 'craft centre') + Gerabah (ID: 'earthenware pottery'); ethno-cultural craft-identity name — the place defined by its Sasak pottery tradition.
DAM (English/Dutch loanword: 'dam/weir') + Keru (Sasak village name, uncertain root); morpho-geographic hydraulic infrastructure name.
Banyu (Javanese: 'water') + Mulek (Sasak/Javanese: 'to revolve', referencing the potter's wheel); formally designated administrative village unit.
Batu ('stone') + Kumbung (Malay/Sasak: 'open-sided granary shed'); formally designated administrative village unit.
Batu Mekar (ID: 'blossoming stone') — aspirational administrative epithet; formally designated.
Batu Putih (Malay/ID: 'white stone/rock'); morpho-geographic geological descriptor used as a village name — white limestone outcrops characterise the area.
Batu ('stone') + Layar (ID: 'sail'); morpho-geographic — references a sail-shaped rock formation; the village is named for the landmark.
Barat (ID: 'west') — cardinal direction suffix added in administrative sub-division of Desa Batulayar; formally designated.
Bengkaung (Sasak: possibly from bengkung 'curved/bent'); formally designated administrative village unit.
Bukit Tinggi (ID: 'high hill'); morpho-geographic height descriptor used as an administrative village name.
Buwun Mas ('golden spring'); formally designated administrative village unit — the morpho-geographic name functions administratively.
Buwun Sejati (ID/Sasak: 'true/genuine spring') — aspirational administrative epithet added to the original toponym; formally designated.
Cendi Manik (Sasak: cendi 'to shine/glitter' + manik 'bead/jewel'); aspirational administrative name; formally designated.
Dasan (Sasak: 'settlement/hamlet') + Geria (Balinese: 'Brahmin house/quarters'); encodes Balinese caste-settlement history; formally designated.
Gegelang (Sasak: from gelang 'bracelet/ring'; reduplicated form); formally designated administrative unit.
Gelangsar (Sasak: 'to slide/slip'); formally designated administrative unit.
Gili Gede (Sasak: 'large small-island') + Indah (ID: 'beautiful') — aspirational epithet appended; formally designated.
Giri (Sanskrit: 'mountain') + Madia (Balinese/Sanskrit: 'middle'); formally designated with Sanskrit heritage naming.
Giri ('mountain') + Sasak (the ethnonym of the indigenous people of Lombok); encodes Sasak ethnic self-identity — ethno-cultural.
Giri ('mountain') + Tembesi (Sasak: a type of hardwood, Fagraea fragrans); formally designated administrative unit.
Golong (Sasak: 'united/gathered together'); formally designated — commemorates the social character of community unity.
Guntur Macan (ID: 'tiger's thunder'); formally designated — power-animal commemorative administrative name.
Jatisela (Javanese: jati 'teak tree' + sela 'gap between rocks'); formally designated with Javanese heritage naming.
Karang (Malay: 'reef/village cluster') + Bayan (Sasak: 'parrot', Eclectus roratus); formally designated — the biotic name functions administratively.
Kebon (Javanese: 'garden/orchard') + Ayu (Javanese/Balinese: 'beautiful'); encodes Javanese agricultural-aesthetic tradition — ethno-cultural.
Kediri — name of an ancient Hindu-Javanese kingdom (Sanskrit-derived); administrative commemorative naming with Hindu-Javanese historical resonance.
Kekait (Sasak: from kait 'to hook/catch'; reduplicated = 'many hooks'); formally designated administrative unit.
Kuranji (Sasak/Malay: possibly from kurangi, a type of plant, Elaeocarpus sp.); formally designated administrative unit.
Kuranji + Dalang (Javanese: 'shadow-puppet master'); encodes the Javanese wayang performance tradition — ethno-cultural.
Kuripan (Sasak: uncertain root) + Induk (ID: 'mother/parent = main settlement'); formally designated administrative unit.
Selatan (ID: 'south') — directional administrative suffix; formally designated.
Duplicate entry in source register — same designation as #43; formally designated.
Langko (Sasak: a type of bamboo or wicker basket); formally designated administrative unit.
Lembah (Malay: 'valley') + Sempage (Sasak: from sempage, uncertain — possibly a type of tree or the sound of rushing water); morpho-geographic valley name used as a village designation.
Lembar (Sasak/Malay: possibly from lembar 'sheet/flat area', referencing flat coastal terrain); formally designated administrative unit.
Selatan ('south') — directional administrative suffix; formally designated.
Mareje (Sasak village name, uncertain root) + Timur (ID: 'east') — directional administrative suffix; formally designated.
Mekarsari (ID: mekar 'to bloom' + sari 'essence/flower'); aspirational administrative name; formally designated.
Same aspirational name applied to a second village in Narmada sub-district; formally designated.
Meninting (Sasak: from tintin/meninting 'to carry on a shoulder pole'); formally designated administrative unit.
Midang (Sasak: 'to stroll/parade'); formally designated administrative unit.
Narmada (Sanskrit: name of a sacred river in India; applied to this settlement by Balinese royalty); formally designated administrative unit.
Nyurlembang (Sasak: nyur 'coconut palm' + lembang 'valley'); formally designated administrative unit.
Pakuan (from pakuan, a type of fern, Stenochlaena palustris); formally designated administrative unit.
Pelangan (Sasak: from pelangan, uncertain root — possibly a type of coastal plant or from pelang 'striped/barred'); formally designated administrative unit.
Penimbung (Sasak: from timbung 'to heap up/accumulate'); formally designated administrative unit.
Pusuk (Sasak: 'summit/top of a pass') + Lestari (ID: 'enduring/sustainable') — conservation epithet appended to morpho-geographic root; formally designated.
Sari (Malay: 'essence/core') + Baya (Malay: 'crocodile/age'); formally designated administrative unit.
Sedau (Sasak: uncertain root, possibly a type of grass or sedge); formally designated administrative unit.
Sekotong (Sasak: possibly kotong 'cut off/isolated') + Barat ('west'); directional administrative suffix; formally designated.
Sekotong + Tengah (ID: 'middle/central'); directional administrative suffix; formally designated.
Senggigi (Sasak toponym of uncertain root, possibly from sengi 'to drift'); formally designated administrative village unit.
Senteluk (Sasak: uncertain root); formally designated administrative unit.
Sesaot (Sasak: possibly from sesaot/saot 'source/spring point'); formally designated administrative unit.
Sesela (Sasak: possibly from sela 'gap/pass between rocks'); formally designated administrative unit.
Suranadi (Sanskrit: sura 'divine/deity' + nadi 'river'); formally designated administrative unit — the Sanskrit religious toponym functions administratively.
Taman Ayu (Javanese/Balinese: taman 'garden' + ayu 'beautiful'); encodes Balinese-Javanese aesthetic-cultural tradition — ethno-cultural.
Tempos (Sasak: uncertain root, possibly seasonal/temporal reference); formally designated administrative unit.

Desert Point — English commercial alias for Pantai Bangko-Bangko; descriptive-natural name invoking the arid, remote, windswept sensory character of the surf break. A neologistic tourism-commercial toponym.
Elak-Elak (Sasak: reduplicated form of elak 'to dodge/avoid'); morpho-geographic coastal name — may reference a zigzag shoreline or wave pattern requiring navigation skill.
Embung (Javanese/ID: 'small reservoir/retention pond') + Kedaro (Sasak village name, uncertain root); morpho-geographic hydrological infrastructure name.
Gili (Sasak: 'small island') + Asahan (Sasak: from asah 'to sharpen'; asahan = 'whetstone'); morpho-geographic — the island's rough stone texture resembles a whetstone.
Gili + Gede (Balinese/Sasak: 'large/great'); morpho-geographic size descriptor — the largest island in the Sekotong group.
Gili + Genting (Malay: 'narrow isthmus/critical point'); morpho-geographic — references the narrow spit connecting two land masses.
Gili + Goleng (Sasak: from goleng/gulung 'to roll/rounded'); morpho-geographic shape descriptor — the island's rounded form.
Gili + Kedis (Sasak: 'bird'); descriptive-natural faunal name — the island characterised by its bird population.
Gili + Layar (Malay: 'sail'); morpho-geographic — references the island's sail-like profile or role as a sailing landmark.
Gili + Lontar (Malay/Sasak: the lontar palm, Borassus flabellifer); descriptive-natural biotic name — the island characterised by its dominant palm species.
Gili + Mas (Malay/ID: 'gold'); descriptive-natural colour/value name — the island named for its golden-sand or golden-light appearance.
Gili + Nanggu (Sasak: possibly a type of coastal vine or rope plant); morpho-geographic island with biotic structural qualifier.
Gili + Poh (Sasak: the mango tree, Mangifera indica); descriptive-natural biotic name — the island characterised by its mango trees.
Gili + Rengit (Sasak: 'mosquito'); descriptive-natural faunal/sensory name — encoding the unpleasant insect character of the island.
Gili + Sudak (Sasak: possibly 'to pierce/spear through'); morpho-geographic — references the island's narrow, pointed landform.
Gili + Tangkong (Sasak: a coastal vine, Caesalpinia bonduc); descriptive-natural biotic name — the island characterised by its dominant creeper species.
Goa (Malay: 'cave') + Alam (ID: 'natural/nature') + Raksasa (Sanskrit/ID: 'giant'); morpho-geographic cave name with a size/scale descriptor.
Goa ('cave') + Lembah (Malay: 'valley') + Sempage (Sasak: uncertain); morpho-geographic cave in a valley.
Gua ('cave') + Jepang (ID: 'Japanese') + Labuan (Malay: 'anchorage/harbour') + Poh (Sasak: 'mango tree'); ethno-cultural historical name commemorating WWII Japanese military cave fortifications.
Gunung (Malay: 'mountain') + Jae (Sasak: 'ginger', Zingiber officinale); morpho-geographic mountain name with biotic qualifier — ginger cultivated or found wild on the slopes.

Gunung + Pengsong (Balinese/Sasak: from pengsong 'to move/migrate'; referencing a mythological story of the Balinese royal family relocating their sacred hill temple); ethno-cultural religious-mythological.
Gunung + Sasak (the ethnonym of the indigenous people of Lombok); ethno-cultural — the mountain named for the Sasak people, encoding ethnic territorial identity.
Hutan Wisata (ID: 'tourism forest') + Pusuk (Sasak: 'summit/top of a pass'); morpho-geographic forested mountain-pass area.
Hutan Wisata + Sesaot (Sasak: possibly 'spring/source point'); morpho-geographic forest at a watershed source area.
Hutan Wisata + Suranadi (Sanskrit: sura 'divine' + nadi 'river'); ethno-cultural — the forest named for its sacred Hindu river, encoding religious significance.

Karang (Malay: 'reef/village cluster') + Bayan (Sasak: 'parrot', Eclectus roratus); ethno-cultural — the parrot holds sacred status in local Waktu Telu tradition; the village is defined by this ritual-faunal identity.
Kebun (ID: 'garden/plantation') + Melon (ID: 'melon'); ethno-cultural agro-economic identity name — the attraction defined by its melon-farming practice.
Kerandangan (Sasak: possibly from kerandang, a type of coastal scrub vegetation); morpho-geographic — the beach/forest named for its characteristic coastal scrub habitat.
Kokok (Sasak: 'stream/small river') + Babak (Sasak: 'stretch/section') + Kediri (village name); morpho-geographic hydrological name — a named river stretch.
Makam (Indonesian: 'tomb/grave') + Batulayar (sail-shaped rock); sacred Islamic tomb of a venerated religious figure (*wali*) — ethno-cultural religious.
Makam ('tomb') + Ilham (Indonesian: 'divine inspiration/revelation'); sacred Islamic tomb of a venerated figure — ethno-cultural religious.
Makam ('tomb') + Keramat (Malay: 'sacred/venerated'); a generic term for a sacred Islamic tomb — ethno-cultural religious.
Makam Keramat + Cemare (Sasak: the casuarina tree, Casuarina equisetifolia); ethno-cultural sacred tomb at a casuarina-lined coastal site.
Makam + Mambalan (Sasak village name, uncertain root); sacred Islamic tomb — ethno-cultural religious.
Mangrove (English loan) + Bagek (Sasak: 'stream mouth/estuary') + Kembar (Malay: 'twin/paired'); morpho-geographic — twin mangrove estuaries.
Mangrove + Tanjung (Malay: 'cape/headland') + Batu ('stone/rock'); morpho-geographic — mangrove forest at a rocky headland.
Masjid Kuno (Indonesian/Malay: 'ancient mosque') + Karang Bayan; ethno-cultural religious heritage — a historical Islamic place of worship, among the oldest mosques on Lombok.
Mata Air (Malay: 'spring/eye of water') + Manggong (Sasak: from manggong, a type of freshwater plant or from 'to gurgle'); morpho-geographic freshwater spring name.
Padang Golf (ID: 'golf course') + Golong (Sasak: 'united/gathered'); administrative-commercial recreation facility designation.
Pantai (Malay/ID: 'beach') + Bange (Sasak: possibly from bange/mbange, a type of coastal grass or the sound of waves); morpho-geographic coastal name.
Pantai + Cemare (Sasak: the casuarina tree, Casuarina equisetifolia); descriptive-natural biotic name — the beach characterised by its casuarina tree line.
Pantai + Duduk (ID/Sasak: 'to sit/sitting'); descriptive-natural name encoding the sensory-experiential quality of the beach — a calm, serene place inviting one to sit and rest.
Pantai + Induk (ID: 'mother/main'); administrative naming — the primary/main beach of the area; functions as a hub designation.
Kuranji Dalang (see Desa Kuranji Dalang #41) — beach named for the Dalang (shadow-puppet master) tradition; ethno-cultural.
Loco (uncertain — possibly from Sasak loko/loco 'bend/curve', referencing the curved bay shape; or from Portuguese/Spanish loco 'wild'); morpho-geographic coastal shape name.
Mangsit (Sasak: 'to beckon/call out'); oral tradition names this as a landmark for sailors — morpho-geographic coastal navigation anchor.
Mekaki (Sasak toponym of uncertain root); morpho-geographic coastal place name for this stretch of shore.
Pantai + Meninting (from the village name; Sasak: possibly from tintin 'to carry'); morpho-geographic coastal name tied to the settlement.
Pantai + Nambung (Sasak: possibly from tambung/nambung 'protruding coastal rock'); morpho-geographic — references the rock formation creating a natural arch/waterfall effect.
Orong (Sasak: 'ravine/gully') + Bukal (Sasak: 'spring/upwelling'); morpho-geographic compound describing a beach at a coastal ravine with freshwater spring.
Pantai + Senggigi (Sasak: uncertain root, possibly from sengi 'to drift/wave'); morpho-geographic coastal name.
Pantai + Sepi (ID: 'quiet/deserted/isolated'); descriptive-natural name encoding the sensory quality of solitude and silence — an auditory-atmospheric descriptor.
Pantai + Tanjung (Malay: 'cape/headland') + Bias (Malay: 'fine sand/filtered light'); morpho-geographic cape name with a textural-optical qualifier.
Pantai + Uringin (Sasak: the weeping fig, Ficus benjamina); morpho-geographic biotic beach name — the beach characterised by its weeping fig trees.
Pao-Pao (Sasak: reduplicated form of pao, 'mango'; Mangifera sp.); descriptive-natural biotic name — the place characterised by an abundance of mango trees.
Pasar Seni (ID: 'art market') + Sesela (Sasak village name); ethno-cultural craft/commercial-identity name — the place defined by its artisan market tradition.
Pelabuhan (Malay: 'port/harbour') + Lembar (Sasak: possibly 'flat area'); morpho-geographic maritime infrastructure name.
Pura (Sanskrit via Balinese: 'Hindu temple') + Agung (Sanskrit/Balinese: 'great/supreme'); ethno-cultural religious — a supreme Hindu temple, encoding Balinese cosmological hierarchy.
Pura + Batu Bolong (Malay: 'hollow rock'); ethno-cultural religious — Hindu temple occupying a geomorphic coastal landmark.
Pura + Lingsar (Sasak: possibly from lingsir 'to decline/set', referencing western orientation); the multi-faith Hindu-Sasak-Waktu Telu-Buddhist temple — ethno-cultural religious.

Purekmas (acronym/branding: Pusat Rekreasi Keluarga Masyarakat, 'Family Community Recreation Centre'); ethno-cultural community-social facility — the name encodes community social structure and collective leisure identity.
Pusuk (Sasak: 'summit/top of mountain pass') + Lestari (ID: 'enduring/sustainable'); morpho-geographic landform name — the pass/summit — with an appended conservation qualifier.

Taman ('garden') + Gandari (Sanskrit: from Gandhari, a queen in the Mahabharata; or from gandha 'fragrance') + Kawari (Sasak: 'special/unique'); ethno-cultural name encoding Hindu literary-cultural heritage.
Taman ('garden/park') + Langit (ID: 'sky'); descriptive-natural name evoking the sky-facing, elevated, open-air sensory experience of this hilltop viewpoint.

Taman + Narmada (Sanskrit: sacred river name, applied by Balinese King Anak Agung Gede Ngurah as spiritual analogy to Mount Rinjani and sacred Balinese rivers); ethno-cultural Hindu royal-religious commemorative.
Taman + Suranadi (Sanskrit: sura 'divine' + nadi 'river'); ethno-cultural religious — the park at the sacred divine river, encoding Hindu cosmological naming.
Tanjung (Malay: 'cape/headland') + Kelor (Malay/ID: the moringa tree, Moringa oleifera); morpho-geographic cape name with a biotic qualifier — moringa trees mark the headland.
Tawun (Sasak: from tawun/tewun, possibly 'a place of gathering' or a type of coastal plant); morpho-geographic coastal settlement name.

Telaga (Malay: 'lake/pond') + Pandan Wangi (Malay: the fragrant pandan, Pandanus amaryllifolius); descriptive-natural name encoding both a water feature and an olfactory quality — the pond characterised by the fragrance of pandan.
Tembowong (Sasak: possibly from tembong 'to appear/emerge' or buwong 'a spring/water hole that surfaces'); morpho-geographic hydrological name — a natural water source that emerges from the ground.
